dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 for cardiac arrest at [CITY].
Hi, I'm Mark and this is my partner Sarah. What made you call 911 today?
She was unresponsive in the car. I think she might have overdosed.
She is not responding to our questions or to light taps on the shoulder.
Her eyes are closed, and she's not responding to verbal stimuli.
She has no verbal response to loud voice or tapping.
No movement or motor response to pain or stimulation observed.
We've confirmed a patent airway but will insert a nasopharyngeal airway now.
Her pulse is 96 and regular-strong peripheral pulses.
She has very shallow breathing. We'll assist with ventilation.
Respiratory rate is 5 per minute-considered bradypneic.
Her oxygen saturation is 81%-signs of hypoxia present.
Her pulse is strong, skin appears normal with no pallor or diaphoresis.
Blood pressure is 144 over 80-within normal range.
Her glucose level is 420-this is significantly elevated.
We are moving forward with BVM-assisted ventilation.
NPA is in place and secured for airway maintenance.
Ventilations via BVM are now in progress.
IV access established in the external jugular for medication delivery.
Could you confirm she has a history of opioid use or any known allergies?
She didn't answer her phone. I found her in the car unresponsive and started CPR.
No signs of trauma, rashes, or visible lesions.
Pupils are pinpoint, approximately 2 mm-consistent with opioid overdose.
No neck stiffness or deformities observed.
She's now tachycardic with a pulse rate of 122-likely due to overdose effects.
Respiration rate increased slightly to 12 per minute-still low, but improved.
Her blood pressure is 138 over 72-no acute change in perfusion.
Oxygen saturation improved to 99%-indicating better oxygenation now.
Her glucose level is now 0-this is critically low and must be monitored continuously.
Circulation is maintained. IV site remains patent and secure.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia-common in opioid-related cases.
Cardiac sounds are normal and regular at this time.
Based on signs and Naloxone response, protocol 7-1 for opioid overdose is active.
Administering 0.1 mg of Narcan IV-first dose.
Second dose of 0.1 mg Narcan administered to increase sedation reversal.
Four-lead ECG in place for continuous cardiac monitoring.
She shows altered mental status. Glasgow Coma Scale is 5 (E2, V1, M2).
End-tidal CO2 is 85-indicating minimal spontaneous breathing effort.
No acute new observations from current vitals.
Her breathing improved with Narcan, but she is still drowsy.
No signs of pain or discomfort reported or observed.
We're transporting her to [CITY], the closest facility offering appropriate care.
She's secured on the stretcher with all monitoring equipment in place.
During transport, vitals remain stable: HR 122, BP 138/72, SpO2 at 99%, glucose 0.
Ma'am, we are on our way to the hospital for further care.
I don't know why I'm here… I took too many pills.
Just stay with us; we're getting you the help you need.
Thank you for your cooperation. You're doing great.
She's opening her eyes to painful stimuli and is confused at this point.
Skin continues to show no abnormalities; chest expansion is equal.
All monitoring in place. Vitals are stable during transport.
Her vitals remain at 122 HR, 138/72 BP, 99 SpO2, and glucose 0.
Naloxone is working, and she remains stable.
Transport complete. Patient is stable at [CITY].
This is Unit 7. Patient transferred to ED with stable vitals.
Transferred successfully. No signs of trauma observed initially.
Arrived with a Glasgow Coma Score of 14 and conscious enough for ED care.
No signs of tachypnea en route. Equipment and lines functional throughout transport.
Four-lead ECG remains securely in place with stable output.
She was securely transported without interventions needed. Vitals at baseline.
Picked up at 23:18 with IV in place. No fractures or large injuries noted.
She regained responsiveness with a GCS of 14. No further decline seen so far.
All stable vitals recorded: 122 HR, 138/72 BP, and 99 SpO2.
Female in her 40s. No height or weight records due to immediate instability.
Non-traumatic collapse confirmed. No external bleeding or signs of injury.
ECG, IV, and oxygenation data throughout transport well captured.
NPA, IV, BVM, 4-lead monitoring, and two MediTags in place for ED reference.
Transport timeline consistent with treatments and documentation.
She's improving as expected following Naloxone-responsive to treatment.
ALS interventions appropriate and aligned with overdose protocol 7-1.
All equipment and condition clearly relayed for continuity at [CITY].
Pathology includes opioid-induced altered mental status and low blood sugar.
All findings from start to ED accurately documented.
No emergency code active-out-of-hospital condition managed at advanced life support.
Patient remained stable and responsive during transport and is now in the ED.
No tachypnea noted-current respiratory rate remains stable at 12 per minute.
Blood pressure is 138/72-no acute signs of hypotension observed.
Altered mental status persists with GCS of 14-no improvement to normal baseline.
